description: In 2017, the Kenyan government implemented one of the world's strictest bans on the manufacturing, sale, and use of plastic carrier bags. The policy, enforced by the National Environment Management Authority (NEMA), faced heavy initial opposition from manufacturers fearing job losses but eventually gained broad public support due to visible reductions in urban drainage blockages and litter.

key_events.description: Kenya Association of Manufacturers files a legal challenge against the Ministry of Environment.
NEMA begins aggressive enforcement, including heavy fines for retailers and shoppers caught with plastic bags.
High Court rules in favor of the ban, dismissing claims that the policy was 'vague and oppressive'.
Visible improvement in urban drainage systems and reduction in plastic waste in Nairobi markets shifts public perception.

calibration_notes: This scenario captures a transition from economic protectionism (industry opposition) to environmental civic pride, providing a model for how extreme regulation policies evolve from unpopular to widely accepted in developing economies.
